title: ## GUIDELINES ON THE JUDGEMENT OF EQUIVALENCE OF SANITARY MEASURES ASSOCIATED WITH FOOD INSPECTION AND CERTIFICATION SYSTEMS 1

sections.section: ## GUIDELINES ON THE JUDGEMENT OF EQUIVALENCE OF SANITARY MEASURES ASSOCIATED WITH FOOD INSPECTION AND CERTIFICATION SYSTEMS 1
sections.summary: Document title and code: Guidelines on the Judgement of Equivalence of Sanitary Measures Associated with Food Inspection and Certification Systems (CAC/GL 53-2003).

sections.section: ## SECTION 1 - PREAMBLE
sections.summary: Preamble explaining that importing and exporting countries may use different food inspection and certification systems, that equivalence determinations (consistent with the WTO SPS Agreement) can be used to assess whether exporting country measures achieve an importing country's Appropriate Level of Protection (ALOP), and that equivalence facilitates trade while protecting consumers; importing countries should avoid unnecessary measures and may reduce verification once equivalence is judged.

sections.section: SECTION 2 - SCOPE
sections.summary: States the document provides guidelines on judging equivalence of sanitary measures associated with food inspection and certification systems and notes measures for equivalence can be broadly characterized as infrastructure, programme design/implementation/monitoring, and/or specific requirements (see paragraph 13).

sections.section: SECTION 3 - DEFINITIONS
sections.summary: Defines key terms used in the guidelines—including ‘sanitary measure’ (covering laws, processes, tests, sampling, labelling related to food safety), ‘hazard’, ‘risk’, ‘risk assessment’, ‘Appropriate Level of Sanitary Protection (ALOP)’, and ‘equivalence’—consistent with Codex and WTO SPS definitions.

sections.section: SECTION 4 - GENERAL PRINCIPLES FOR THE DETERMINATION OF EQUIVALENCE
sections.summary: Sets out principles for determining equivalence: the importing country may set its ALOP and must describe how its measures achieve it; importing countries should recognize different measures that can achieve the ALOP and promptly consult on requests; the exporting country must objectively demonstrate its measures achieve the ALOP; comparisons must be objective and, where appropriate, use risk assessment and international methods; determinations should consider prior experience, allow inspection/access, ensure maintenance of equivalence, be transparent and cooperative, and provide technical assistance to developing exporters.

sections.section: SECTION 5 - THE CONTEXT OF AN EQUIVALENCE DETERMINATION
sections.summary: Explains the context for equivalence determinations: Codex-based sanitary measures are encouraged; equivalence may be sought for any measure or set of measures; the extent of review depends on the importing country's prior experience and confidence, and measures not under determination should still meet importing requirements; it categorizes measures as (a) infrastructure, (b) programme design/implementation/monitoring, and (c) specific requirements with examples to facilitate comparisons.

sections.section: ## SECTION 6 - OBJECTIVE BASIS OF COMPARISON
sections.summary: States that an exporting country can demonstrate achievement of the importing country's ALOP by showing proposed measures have the same effect relative to that ALOP using an objective basis of comparison, and that the importing country should, on request, specify that objective basis and may provide supporting information (purpose, relation to ALOP, level of control, scientific basis, and other relevant data).

sections.section: 12.  When  an  importing ...
sections.summary: Notes that when an importing country lacks prior experience, knowledge and confidence regarding the exporting country's relevant food control measures (e.g., first-time trade), determining equivalence will require further consideration of the other measures comprising the exporting country's food control system.

sections.section: ## SECTION 8 - JUDGEMENT
sections.summary: Specifies that judgements of equivalence should be transparent, objective and consistent with consultation, and should consider factors such as the exporting country's experience and supporting data, strength of the relationship between measures and the importing country's ALOP, quantitative parameters where possible, qualitative adequacy, data uncertainty, expected human health outcomes, and relevant Codex texts; parties should promptly notify each other of significant post‑determination changes.

sections.section: APPENDIX
sections.summary: Appendix heading indicating additional guidance is provided to assist exporting and importing countries in undertaking equivalence determinations of sanitary measures associated with food inspection and certification systems.

sections.section: ADDITIONAL GUIDANCE TO ASSIST EXPORTING AND IMPORTING COUNTRIES IN UNDERTAKING AN EQUIVALENCE DETERMINATION OF SANITARY MEASURES
sections.summary: States the Appendix offers additional guidance clarifying aspects of the Guidelines, covering preliminary considerations, facilitating factors, preparatory steps, scoping, experience/knowledge/confidence, and development of an objective basis of comparison for equivalence determinations.

sections.section: PRELIMINARY CONSIDERATIONS RELATING TO UNDERTAKING AN EQUIVALENCE DETERMINATION
sections.summary: Explains preliminary considerations: equivalence requests span a spectrum from measures for a food group to single sanitary measures, and factors that may facilitate determination include importing country's experience/confidence, trade history, compliance levels, authority cooperation, system similarity, and access to scientific/technical resources.

sections.section: 2. There is a broad spectrum of circumstances where an exporting country may wish to seek an equivalence determination with an importing country. While each circumstance will  likely  need  to  be  considered  on  a  case-by-case  basis,  it  can  vary  from  seeking equivalence for a set of sanitary measures making up a food control system associated with  a  certain  type  of  food  or  group  of  foods  (e.g.  dairy  products)  to  seeking equivalence for a sanitary measure (e.g. analytical method).
sections.summary: States that equivalence requests vary widely and should be considered case‑by‑case, ranging from sets of sanitary measures for food groups (e.g., dairy) to individual measures such as analytical methods.

sections.section: 3. Factors that may facilitate the equivalence determination of sanitary measures could include the following:
sections.summary: Lists factors that may facilitate equivalence determinations, including the importing country's experience/knowledge/confidence in the exporting country's system, prior trade history, compliance records, cooperation between competent authorities, similarity of control systems, and availability of necessary resources.

sections.section: Preparatory steps to undertaking an equivalence determination
sections.summary: Lists preparatory steps for undertaking an equivalence determination: the exporting country should assess benefits, costs and resource implications, consider priority-setting guidance, ensure access to scientific and technical resources, and, where appropriate, develop a plan with objectives, milestones, timelines and expected outcomes.

sections.section: ## GUIDANCE ON UNDERTAKING AN EQUIVALENCE DETERMINATION
sections.summary: Provides guidance on undertaking equivalence determinations with emphasis on scoping the request (identifying sanitary measures and commodity combinations), deciding which importing measures to meet versus seek equivalence for, and using categorization to clarify the scope and organise comparisons.

sections.section: Scoping the equivalence determination
sections.summary: Explains how an exporting country should scope a request for an equivalence determination by identifying the sanitary measures and food commodity combination, deciding which importing measures it will meet by direct compliance versus seek equivalence for, and using categorization of measures (paras. 13–14) to organize comparisons and identify the measures subject to determination.

sections.section: 5.  The  exporting  country  should  appropriately  scope  the  request  for  an  equivalence  determination  by identifying the sanitary measures and food commodity combination to be submitted for consideration.
sections.summary: Advises that the exporting country should appropriately scope its equivalence request by identifying the sanitary measures and the food commodity combination to be considered.

sections.section: 6. The exporting country must decide on which of the importing country's measures it will meet by compliance and for which measures it will seek equivalence.
sections.summary: Requires the exporting country to decide which of the importing country's measures it will meet by direct compliance and which measures it will seek to meet via an equivalence determination.

sections.section: 7. In some situations it will be clear as to the specific measure or group of measures that are the subject of the equivalence determination.
sections.summary: Notes that in some cases it will be clear which specific measure or group of measures are the subject of the equivalence determination.

sections.section: 8. In other situations the scope of the equivalence determination may not be clear and categorization  of  sanitary  measures  as  referred  to  in  paragraphs  13  and  14  of  the Guidelines  may  assist  in  determining  the  scope  of  the  equivalence  determination. Specifically, categorisation may assist with organising sanitary measures, carrying out side-by-side comparisons of those measures where appropriate, and identifying which measures will be the subject of the equivalence determination.
sections.summary: States that when the scope of an equivalence determination is unclear, categorization of sanitary measures (per paras. 13–14) can help organize measures, enable side-by-side comparisons where appropriate, and identify which measures should be subject to detailed examination.

sections.section: Experience, knowledge and confidence
sections.summary: Provides guidance on what constitutes an importing country's 'experience, knowledge and confidence' in an exporting country's food inspection and certification system—listing evidence such as trade and compliance history, side-by-side comparisons, audits/inspections, application of risk analysis, import inspection results, existing agreements, changes in competent authorities, contingency plans, surveillance data, processing controls, legislation and oversight, pre-existing certification and export control systems—and notes this information can be applied at any stage to facilitate review, reduce the scope or evidence needed, assist prioritization and comparisons, and must be used transparently.
